textPlain: In the early 2000s, growing up in a third-world country with limited resources meant computers and operating systems were constantly breaking. That scarcity pushed me to tinker and experiment, I learned to troubleshoot hardware, reinstall OSes, and reverse-engineer odd behaviors. I even experimented with keyloggers out of curiosity. That practical, trial-and-error schooling is where a lot of the so called “common sense” about security comes from. It is less theory, more failing, fixing, and learning what actually keeps one safe online.I think it all stemmed from curiosity to learn and tinker. I wonder if gamifying it is enough but it’s a step.

Game for kids, where you dedicate a third of the screen to a locked hint list and a very prominent "Buy Hint Tokens" button? Hard pass.https://www.hacktivate.app/img/framed-ipad-3.pngThe game industry needs to move away from milking vulnerable people with pay-to-win schemes.
Neat... Brings memories of the national cybersecurity courses you were talking about.I never figured out how to do that "cat flag" terminal privilege escalation.

Early 90's were more fun. I modified DOS command.com file to change the outputs it prints, drilled holes into laptop to attach broken hinges, break electronic garbage to salvage wires and interesting things, disassemble disk drives, ...
I suspect gamifying it isn't enough, but as you say it's a step, and if it helps more people get involved then hopefully others can provide more steps to follow.
Yeah, sorry; I know my limitations, and would rather do one thing very well than two things kinda average.
